free_text: Neutron yield from carbon, light-and heavy-
water thick targets irradiated by 40 MeV deuterons.
K-130 cyclotron at Jyvaeskylae
The calibration of the current integrator was
checked by replacing the Faraday cup with an external
well-calibrated 2 nA current source.
The currents obtained in these two methods were
reproduced using a cross-section AL-27(D,P+A)11-NA-24
of 24.6 mb
Details of the target chamber
The target has both length and diameter of 15 mm.
The aluminium wall and entrance window of the container
are 0.5 mm thick. The targets were either a graphite
powder of density 1.76, heavy water or light water.
.A coaxial Ge-detector .
Errors on the conversion factor are thus quasi-fully
defined by the cross-sections, without bias from
experimental conditions.This defines neutron yields to
about 15% accuracy.The agreement between both methods
was very good(TOF and ACTIV).After these tests, it
turned out that the scale of the original measurements
had to be multiplied with factors of 1.07(4) for the
carbon,  0.87(6)for the heavy water and 0.94(9) for the
light-water targets. These factors give an estimate of
the systematical errors on relative yield measurements,
i.e. 8% for heavy water to carbon and 11% for light
water to carbon.
*By author*.All targets have been irradiated with close
to 1 nA positive deuterium ions during 14 hr,during,
which the foils have been exposed to the neutron flux
generated by the targets.
*By author*. The carbon target was irradiated first in
order to validate the method by comparison with time-of
flight data. Irradiations of heavy-and light-water
targets were performed several months later.

*By authors* The spectra were therefore stretched
(resp.compressed) to the end-point energy of 40 MeV,
normalized to a common value interpolated according to
the distance of the actual beam energy to 40 MeV and
the average was calculated.
